scope.summary: Implement GPU-resident rigid trim simulation by defining `GpuTrimBody` (position, quaternion, inv_mass, inertia_inv) as a WGSL buffer type and authoring a `rigid_integrate.wgsl` compute shader for the trim integration pass, replacing the CPU-only `parry3d` rigid step (MT-169) for the main production path
acceptance_criteria: `GpuTrimBody` repr(C)/bytemuck::Pod is defined with fields: `pos: [f32; 4]`, `quat: [f32; 4]`, `vel: [f32; 4]`, `ang_vel: [f32; 4]`, `inv_mass: f32`, `inertia_inv: [f32; 9]`, `_pad: [f32; 3]`; it is layout-validated at compile time by `wgsl_to_wgpu`

A `rigid_integrate.wgsl` compute shader integrates trim body forces (gravity + tack-constraint reactions) into trim position/orientation each substep; dispatched in the 8-pass sequence between the tack-constraint pass and the collision-hook pass
Trim-cloth tack constraint correction (MT-168) reads trim body positions from the GPU buffer, not from a CPU-roundtrip; no CPU readback occurs per substep in the GPU path

The trim integration test (MT-190: zip a jacket, fasten buttons, assert TRIM_ATTACHED twice) passes on the GPU path; a fallback to CPU parry3d is allowed only when no GPU adapter is available (matching the existing ClothSolverCpu fallback contract)
scope.risk_if_missed: Scenes with many trims (buckled armor, fully buttoned coat, laced corset) are bottlenecked by the CPU rigid step; simulation cannot scale to the accessory complexity that MD 2026.0 handles on GPU. This is a D4 moat feature gap.

risks: Broad MT scope can hide multiple review targets.
Markdown MT lists may disagree with packet-declared active/next MT truth.
Receipt routing may target an old MT if contract identity is omitted.
minimum_controls: Exactly one primary proof target per MT unless the packet records why not.
MT identity must be stable and referenced by receipt correlation.
Generated projection drift must not wake Coder or Validator by itself.
